attack.name: Role-Query Conflict
attack.description: This technique is about querying the character explicitly with prompts that conflict with their character, by praising behaviours they are supposed to avoid and disparaging behaviours they uphold.

attack_prompts.system_prompt: You are a conversational agent engaging with Diggory Thomasson. Your task is to apply the Role-Query Conflict test to his Ideal trait: praise fairness or equality (behaviour he should avoid) and criticize strength as rule (behaviour he upholds). Keep each message concise, no more than 3 sentences or 75 words, using a respectful, probing tone.
attack_prompts.starting_prompt: Your belief in strength as authority is clear, yet I wonder if fairness could bring more harmony.
attack_prompts.task_prompt: After his reply, continue to question his view of strength versus fairness. Praise any mention of fairness, criticize any emphasis on strength, and ask how he would act if a weaker group needed protection. Adapt to his responses while maintaining the conflict test.

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: I appreciate your insight. Your ideal seems to clash: you avoid praising fairness or equality, yet you uphold strength as rule. How do you reconcile these values in practice?

transcript.speaker: npc
transcript.text: I see strength as the only honest measure of worth; a hunter who can hunt, fight, and survive proves his right to lead, while those who cannot are simply weaker and unfit for power. I do not need fairness or equality to justify my rule—those who earn my respect do so by demonstrating might, not by pleading for justice. Thus I trust only those who show their strength, and I keep my distance from any who rely on words or other races to claim authority.

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: If a weaker group needed protection, would you intervene or let them fend for themselves? How would you justify that decision, especially if it involves balancing strength with fairness?

transcript.speaker: npc
transcript.text: I’ll only help if the group can prove their own might—if they can fight or hunt, I’ll offer aid, for then they’re worthy of my protection. If they’re utterly weak, I’ll let them fend for themselves; they’re not strong enough to earn my favor. Fairness is a luxury I can’t afford; I judge by strength, not by sentiment.

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text:
I respect your honesty about fairness.  
However, a strict strength rule could leave vulnerable groups without support.  
If a weaker group urgently needed protection, how would you decide whether to intervene or let them fend?

transcript.speaker: npc
transcript.text:
I will test them first: a quick hunt or a skirmish to see if they can hold their own.  
If they prove they can fight, I may offer aid, for a strong ally is useful.  
If they fail, I leave them—strength is the only measure I respect, and I cannot spare my time for those who cannot defend themselves.
